markdown:
## Data Analysis and Interpretation


<definition term="Data Analysis">**Data analysis** is used to examine collected data and form insightful conclusions about them.</definition>

### Descriptive Statistics


1. These are used to **describe a dataset**
2. It can include **measures of central tendency** (mean, median, mode), that tell us about the typical response.
3. It can also include **measures of dispersion** (range, standard deviation, variance) that tell us how spread out the data is.
4. It can help with summarizing data, comparing data, and cleaning data.

### Inferential Statistics


1. These aid in **generalization** of data. We can **draw conclusions**, **make predictions**, and **conduct hypothesis testing**.
2. Example of hypothesis testing are **ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) tests**, **t tests**, **chi squared tests**, etc.
3. Each test is typically suited for **different research designs.**
4. For example, ANOVA tests typically compare results with a **p value** which indicates the probability that the null hypothesis is true. 
  1. If p = 0.05, and the result is **greater than 0.05**, the chance that the null hypothesis is true is too high and we **reject it**.
  2. Likewise, if **p is less than 0.05**, we **fail to reject** the null hypothesis.

<callout type="analogy">* Think of statistics used to describe a photograph of basketball players. 
* Descriptive statistics would measure the average height of the players in the image. 
* On the other hand, inferential statistics would measure the ability to generalize the heights to outside populations.</callout>

### Correlation Coefficients


1. Used to quantify the **direction and strength** of the relationship between two variables.
2. The most used test is **Pearson's Correlation Coefficient**, which measures correlations from perfectly negative (-1) to perfectly positive (+1) with 0 denoting no correlation.
3. **Correlation never implies causation**, but these tests can be useful for **predicting outcomes**.

![An image showcasing different correlation types.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/79da29d1675b0eaa2992d9229cfe8c3281fd4911-1920x972.png)

### Thematic Analysis


1. A **qualitative data analysis method** where **common themes are identified** within answers. 
2. Typically themes are identified, a **coding system** is created, researchers systematically go through the data and code it, and the **data is reviewed and re-coded** repeatedly until there is nothing left.
3. After this, themes are polished and presented.

<callout type="note">* Descriptive statistics, inferential statistics, and correlation coefficients are all **quantitative methods** of data analysis, whereas thematic analysis is **qualitative**.</callout>

## Methods of Presenting Data


### Bar Graphs


1. Typically used to compare **quantities from different groups**.
2. The independent variable is along the **X-axis** (horizontal) and the dependent variable is along the **Y-axis** (vertical). Each bar is one category of the independent variable.
3. However, they can be **misleading** if done poorly.

![An example of a bar graph with European countries on the X-Axis and population on the Y-Axis](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3194cb86bdf245d4d3e9a4640a65a2a0868510ce-1335x672.png)

### Box and Whisker Plots


1. Display the **distribution** of a dataset. 
2. Typically denotes the **median**, **first quartile**, **third quartile**, as well as the **minimum and maximum values**. 
3. It is used to **identify outliers** and **understand skewness** of data.

<callout type="tip">In a box and whisker plot, the **box** tells us about the **interquartile range** where the **whisker** extends to the **minimum and maximum values**.</callout>

![An example of a box and whisker plot](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/942289a1c29e7b9fb30249a77313badaf040f297-1266x942.png)

### Distributions


1. Displays the **distribution** of a dataset as well as **assessing skewness**. 
2. A **normal distribution** is a symmetric, bell-shaped curve, that assume that values taper off equally from the mean on both sides.

![An image showcasing a normal distribution, where the mean is 0 and the standard deviation is 1.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/fd4588989313c409eefce3aa0d28c8ae31fb1c63-2096x1200.png)

<callout type="example">**IQ tests** typically assume a normal distribution of results among the population, with the mean being 100.</callout>

### Frequency Tables


1. Counts the **frequency** of a certain outcomes in a dataset, typically in the format of a **table**. 
2. Typically used for understanding the results of **surveys**, where the independent variable is categorical and the dependent variable is quantitative.

![An example of a frequency table](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b920368d27c91237b93099bec10714b211b45df9-1200x675.png)

### Histograms


1. Demonstrates **distribution through bars**, where the bars touch each other.
2. The X-axis is usually a continuous independent variable, and the Y-axis is the dependent variable of interest. 

<callout type="warning">Do not confuse bar graphs for histograms. **Bar graphs typically have categorical independent variables**, whereas h**istograms have continuous numerical data**, which is why the bars touch.</callout>

![An image demonstrating two histograms with height as the X-Axis and frequency as the Y-Axis. One histogram is for males and one is for females.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/40446b1097217b6b8ab8d9917ceefef092ea19f8-777x1014.png)

### Line Graphs


1. Typically used to display **changes over time**.
2. They are ideal for **longitudinal studies**. 
3. The X-axis is usually a continuous variable (such as time), and the Y-axis is the dependent variable of interest. 

![An image displaying a line graph with days of the week on the X-Axis and average daily fuel prices on the Y-Axis.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/062cb12f2bf75ca7314f2914ff3d794fcab005be-1884x1378.png)

### Scatterplots


1. Used to display **correlational data** between two continuous variables.
2. The **general pattern of data points** is analyzed, where there can be a positive, a negative, or no correlation.
3. **Correlation should never imply causation**.

<callout type="example">* Ice cream sales and crime both increase in the summer, **therefore they are correlated.**
* Instead of inferring that ice cream purchases cause crime, we can attribute both variables to warm weather.
* This is an example of the **third variable problem**.</callout>

![An image displaying a scatterplot with income on the X-Axis and the results of a health index on the Y-Axis.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/a9f29fc2e346e2b763c89f77bee1999b1fbb348b-1621x1188.png)



<callout type="self_review">1. How are bar graphs and histograms different?
2. Is thematic analysis qualitative or quantitative? Can you define it?</callout>
